title: Asparagus soup
subtitle: Fresh, grassy, and vivid green
excerpt: British asparagus simmered briefly in stock, blended with a little cream and lemon. A seasonal soup that makes the most of asparagus when it is at its best in May and June. Ready in under 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Asparagus soup is a standard British spring recipe, timed to the British asparagus season (late April to June). Appears in most British cookery books from the 19th century onward.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten immediately. The colour and flavour fade noticeably within a few hours.

glossaryTerms.definition: The natural breaking point of an asparagus spear, where the tender stalk meets the tough, woody base. Bending the spear until it snaps finds this point automatically. The woody end is discarded or used for stock.

content.text: Asparagus soup is best made during the six-week British asparagus season (late April to June) when the spears are at their most flavourful. The method is simple: sweat a little onion in butter, add the asparagus and stock, cook briefly, and blend. The key is not overcooking ,  the same rule that applies to watercress applies here. Tip the woody ends of the asparagus, found by
each spear, can be simmered in the stock first to extract their flavour before discarding.

items.prepNote: woody ends snapped off and discarded

content.text: Snap the woody ends from the {{asparagus}} of asparagus spears. Reserve the tips from 8 spears to garnish ,  these will be blanched separately. Cut the remaining asparagus into rough lengths.
Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 6 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock and bring to a simmer. Add the chopped asparagus stalks (not the reserved tips). Cook for 6 minutes until the asparagus is completely tender.
Meanwhile, blanch the reserved asparagus tips in a small pan of boiling salted water for 2 minutes. Drain and set aside.
Transfer the soup to a jug blender and blend until smooth. Stir in {{double-cream}} of cream and the juice of {{lemon}} lemon. Taste and season with salt.
Ladle into bowls. Place the blanched asparagus tips in the centre of each bowl and add a small swirl of cream.

items.symptom: Soup is dull rather than vivid green
items.cause: The asparagus was cooked for too long before blending.
items.fix: Six minutes at a simmer is enough for tender asparagus. Blend immediately after cooking. Adding a handful of raw spinach leaves to the blender boosts the colour if it has faded.

items.cause: Asparagus has fibrous strands, especially near the cut ends.
items.fix: Blend for at least 60 seconds in a jug blender. Pass through a fine-mesh sieve for a completely smooth result.

items.cause: Out-of-season asparagus has much less flavour than May or June-harvested British spears.
items.fix: Add a small knob of butter to each bowl at serving. Season generously. The lemon juice is important: without it the flavour stays flat.

items.cause: Asparagus gives very little body without a starch addition.
items.fix: If a thicker soup is wanted, add one small peeled and diced potato to the base at the same time as the stock and cook through before blending.

content.text: British asparagus has been celebrated since at least the 17th century, when London market traders sold it by the bundle. The Vale of Evesham in Worcestershire and the Wye Valley are its main growing regions. The British season runs for six weeks from late April to late June. As a soup, asparagus is a classic dinner-party starter: it looks impressive, is straightforward to make, and is deeply seasonal.
